query: Angular NgModules vs standalone interview question

selection.greatFrontendReasonIfOmitted: No single-topic GreatFrontEnd page was confirmed for this exact Angular prompt; the available Angular basics coverage is broader than the question-specific comparison needed here.

competitors.label: Angular NgModules Overview
competitors.selectionReason: The official NgModules overview is the canonical baseline for what modules group, export, and still contribute in Angular applications.
theirStrengths: It states the official role of NgModules and how they organize declarations, imports, and exports.
theirGaps: It is not framed around the modern interview question of what NgModules still solve after standalone became normal.

realWorldUseCases: NgModules still matter mainly as a packaging + compatibility layer for legacy declarables and module-based libraries/config APIs.
actionableExamples: Greenfield standalone feature: no new feature NgModule required
Decision matrix: when NgModules are still solving a real problem

competitors.winReason: The official docs remain the mechanics baseline, but our page now answers the interview prompt more directly with a question-shaped decision matrix and concrete standalone-versus-compatibility examples.

realWorldUseCases: The official overview explains NgModule mechanics, but our page is more direct about the remaining real-world reasons teams still keep modules.
actionableExamples: Angular.dev includes concrete module patterns, so the example depth remains close.
followUpConfusion: The docs answer import/export mechanics, but they are less explicit about the standalone-era decision boundary.
Keep the page explicit that NgModule compatibility can survive even when new feature-level NgModule creation is no longer necessary.

competitors.label: Angular University Standalone Components Complete Guide
competitors.selectionReason: This guide is a strong tutorial competitor because it explains why standalone components replaced much NgModule ceremony and how migration works in practice.
theirStrengths: It goes deep on standalone components, migration, and why they reduce NgModule overhead.
theirGaps: It is more about why standalone wins than about the narrower question of what problems NgModules still solve.

realWorldUseCases: NgModules still matter mainly as a packaging + compatibility layer for legacy declarables and module-based libraries/config APIs.
followUpConfusion: Module compatibility means Angular can still interoperate with a module-shaped dependency or legacy declarable. Module necessity means your own new feature still needs a fresh NgModule.

competitors.winReason: Angular University is richer as a migration article, but our page is now better calibrated for the narrower follow-up interview question: when NgModules still solve a real problem after standalone is normal.

realWorldUseCases: Angular University discusses the practical benefits of standalone components and when NgModules add overhead, so the use-case framing is close.
actionableExamples: It includes more worked standalone-versus-module migration examples than our compact trivia answer.
followUpConfusion: It covers interoperability and lazy-loading well, but it is less narrowly organized around the remaining legitimate NgModule use cases.

actionableExamples: If this comparison still feels close, add one more explicit example of bridging a legacy declarable export versus importing a module-shaped library provider API.

competitors.label: Telerik Best of Both Angular Worlds: Standalone & Modules Combined
competitors.selectionReason: This article is a practical competitor because it explicitly discusses mixing standalone components with existing module-based Angular applications.
theirStrengths: It explains coexistence between standalone and module-based code with concrete migration-oriented examples.
theirGaps: It is more implementation-oriented than interview-oriented about the small remaining problem set NgModules still solve.

actionableExamples: Greenfield standalone feature: no new feature NgModule required
That is library compatibility, not proof that your feature still needs its own NgModule.
followUpConfusion: Packaging convenience is not the same thing as a required Angular module boundary.

competitors.winReason: Telerik covers coexistence well, but our page now has the sharper example set and the cleaner compatibility-versus-necessity framing that this interview prompt actually tests.

realWorldUseCases: Telerik frames the coexistence story well, so the practical comparison stays close.
actionableExamples: Its article shows app.config.ts, provideRouter, and module imports concretely, keeping the example criterion close.
followUpConfusion: Our page answers the likely follow-up more directly by naming the narrow cases where NgModules still win and where they no longer should be created.

realWorldUseCases: Keep one explicit sentence on legacy declarables and module-shaped library APIs as the two main surviving module use cases.
